fields.Text: {{c1::Pronation (in pitching)}} - {{c2::rotation of the forearm that turns the palm from up to down (arm held out in front, like turning a tray over)}}
fields.Back Extra: In a pitching delivery, the forearm naturally pronates as the arm decelerates after release. A changeup is thrown by pronating early and deliberately, which produces arm-side fade.

fields.Text: {{c1::Supination (in pitching)}} - {{c2::rotation of the forearm that turns the palm from down to up (arm held out in front, like turning a tray face-up); the opposite of pronation}}
fields.Back Extra: A slider is thrown by holding a supinated forearm through release. Supinated pitches are generally considered harder on the elbow, partly because the pitcher fights the arm's natural tendency to pronate.

fields.Front: What are the three main categories of baseball pitch?
fields.Back: Fastballs, breaking balls, and offspeed pitches (changeups).

fields.Text: {{c1::Fastballs}} are pitches that {{c2::win on velocity — thrown with the hand behind the ball and minimal manipulation, so they reach the hitter as fast as possible}}.
{{c1::Breaking balls}} are pitches that {{c2::win on spin-driven movement — the wrist is deliberately altered at release to bend the ball's path, which costs velocity}}.
{{c1::Offspeed pitches}} ({{c3::changeups}}) are pitches that {{c2::mimic fastball arm action but arrive slower, fooling the hitter's timing}}.

{{c1::Glove-side}} movement is horizontal break toward the side of the mound where the pitcher's {{c1::glove hand}} is — i.e. away from the throwing arm. It is the opposite of {{c1::arm-side}} movement.
fields.Back Extra: For a right-handed pitcher, glove-side break moves the ball toward first base / away from a right-handed hitter (in on a lefty). Sliders and cutters break glove-side.

fields.Text: {{c1::Arm-side}} movement is horizontal break toward the side of the pitcher's {{c1::throwing}} arm. It is the opposite of {{c2::glove-side}} movement.
fields.Back Extra: For a right-handed pitcher, arm-side break moves the ball toward third base / in on a right-handed hitter. Sinkers and changeups run arm-side.

fields.Text: A “{{c1::changeup}}” is another name for {{c2::an offspeed pitch}}.
For a right-handed pitcher, {{c1::arm-side}} break moves the ball toward third base / in on a right-handed hitter. {{c2::Sinkers}} and {{c3::changeups}} run {{c1::arm-side}}.

For a right-handed pitcher, {{c1::glove-side}} break moves the ball toward first base / away from a right-handed hitter (in on a lefty). {{c2::Sliders}}, {{c3::cutters}}, and {{c4::sweepers}} break {{c1::glove-side}}.
